serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stir-fried noodles with shrimps is approximately 350 g, typically served as a single-plate main course.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Noodles with Prawns (Char Kway Teow / Stir-fried Noodle reference) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for stir-fried noodle dish with seafood
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for stir-fried noodle dishes with seafood (closest match: fried noodles with prawns) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles and shrimp. Per-100g values represent a composite estimate for a typical stir-fried noodle dish with shrimps as served in a Singapore Chinese restaurant context.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles, stir-fried, analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for stir-fried noodles with shrimps. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wheat noodles (boiled/stir-fried) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which list wheat noodles at GI ~52 (low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 70.7) / 100 ≈ 36.9, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 20.2 × 3.5 = 70.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single serving can contribute approximately 73% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~36.9 per serving) from wheat noodle carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size, as the moderate glycemic load of approximately 36.9 per standard serving can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and reducing portion to half a plate may help manage blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from shrimps
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (~1680 mg per serving) and moderate cholesterol from shrimps (~252 mg per serving) warrant portion control and preference for preparation methods using minimal oil and reduced soy sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Noodles with Shrimps (虾仁炒面, Xiā rén chǎo miàn) is a classic Chinese stir-fried noodle dish widely served in Singapore Chinese restaurants and hawker centres. The dish consists of wheat-based egg noodles or yellow noodles wok-tossed over high heat with fresh or frozen shrimps (prawns), garlic, spring onions, bean sprouts, and eggs, seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The cooking technique — rapid stir-frying in a very hot wok — imparts a characteristic smoky 'wok hei' flavour and produces noodles with a slightly chewy texture and a savoury, umami-rich taste. The shrimps contribute a sweet, briny flavour and a firm, succulent texture. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~148 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from shrimps (~7.8 g per 100 g), and carbohydrates primarily from the noodles (~20.2 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), making it a moderate glycemic load meal when consumed in standard restaurant portions. Sodium content is high (~480 mg per 100 g), which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains common allergens including gluten (wheat noodles), crustaceans (shrimps), eggs, and soy. It is generally considered halal when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo stir-fried noodle with seafood entries and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for component ingredients. No single exact entry for 'stir-fried noodles with shrimps' was found in a primary approved source; values represent a defensible composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish type. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this composite estimate. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero / trace) consistent with typical vegetable-oil-based stir-fry preparations. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from typical vegetable oil composition used in stir-frying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 70.7) / 100 ≈ 36.9, based on a 350 g serving containing approximately 70.7 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high due to soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of this dish. The dish is served at Bei Jiang Restaurant 北疆饭店 (Boat Quay, Singapore) at SGD 19.90 per the field survey source record.
